text:
Australia's athletes are arriving to a hero's welcome in Sydney following the country's most successful Olympics, with loved ones and special guests greeting the contingent in a special ceremony.

Australia's best athletes have been given a well-deserved hero's welcome home after making the country proud during their history-making performance at the Olympics.

Australia had its most successful Games, winning a record 18 gold medals, 19 silver, and 16 bronze across 13 sports over the more than two-week event in Paris.

It was an incredible feat from the contingent of 467 athletes to finish fourth overall in the world, with the United States topping the medal tally on 40 gold, tied with China, with first place decided by the number of silvers, and Japan in third.

After letting their hair down at Sunday's glitzy Closing Ceremony - where Paris handed the torch to Los Angeles for the 2028 Games - some of the Australian sporting superstars boarded a 17-hour Qantas flight from the French capital bound for Perth before flying to Sydney for a special welcome home ceremony on Wednesday morning.

The Boeing 787 Flying Kangaroo, which had an Olympic livery with "Go Australia" emblazoned on the aircraft, touched down in the Harbour City just before 7am.

The flight arrived into a wet Sydney earlier than expected, as the aircraft sat outside the hangar waiting some 30 minutes for clearance by air traffic control to enter.

Hundreds of the athletes' loved ones and special guests packed into Qantas' Hangar 96 waiting for their arrival, with the mood upbeat despite the grey skies above.

One such guest was New South Wales Premier Chris Minns, who told Sky News Australia the nation's representatives had "given the whole country a lift", as he hailed their return.

"It's so exciting, I've seen a lot of the parents this morning they're just happy to see their kids return from Europe," he said.

"They're coming back as heroes and they've just given the whole state, the whole country a lift over the last couple of weeks after a tough year."

The first athletes to emerge from the plane to the cheers of their loved ones were the closing ceremony flagbearers and gold medallists sailor Matt Wearn and swimmer Kaylee McKeown.

A girl's choir group sung the iconic Peter Allen song I Still Call Australia Home as they disembarked one-by-one and into the arms of family and friends.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and Australian Governor-General Samantha Mostyn were at the bottom of the steps to shake hands and congratulate each of the athletes.

Media swarmed some of the biggest stars including canoeist Jess Fox, swimmers Cam McEvoy and Emma McKeon and skateboarding sensation Ariana Trew.

The Australian Olympians who were lucky enough to win a medal proudly wore it around their neck.

Skating gold medallist Keegan Palmer told Sky News Australia the victorious homecoming felt "surreal" as he reflected on the difference from the last games.

"We kinda all won our medals and disappeared off the face of the earth, you know? It was just back to normal life right after Tokyo," he said.

"So this is a pretty surreal feeling, you know, it's, like, incredible."

His words were echoed by Australian swimmer Kaylee McKeown, who reflected on another successful games in which she won two gold, a sliver and two bronze medals.

"We're all pretty exhausted, but excited," she also told Sky News Australia.

"Couldn't get much better than the atmosphere they had in Paris, I've never been in something like that before so it's pretty cool to basically be a little ant on pool deck just swimming my little heart out."

With the games now done and dusted, however, McKeown stressed her full focus was on getting a well deserved rest.

Speaking to the crowd after the athletes had fully disembarked, Mr Albanese hailed the success of Team Australia, whether they won a medal or not.

"Every single one of these athletes has done us proud," he said.

"As the athletes went down the stairs there, not everyone had a medal around their neck. But every single one of them was worth their weight in gold."

In a later interview with Sky News Australia, the Prime Minister added the games had been "uplifting" for Australia, as he lauded the conduct of the athletes and their engagement with the nation.

"They've done Australia proud, our most successful team ever, but the character of the team really shone through," he said.

"The way that they've engaged, the embracing of their families and friends and supporters and volunteers who are here, it's an incredible day."

On suggestions he might not still be in office when its Australia's turn to host the games in 2032, Mr Albanese declared "anything is possible", before backing the nation to host the best edition yet.

"It'll be great," he said, dismissing concerns over infrastructure that have dogged the Queensland government in recent months.

"Australia will get it done, it's what we do."
